input_text:
Voyager 1 embarked on its journey from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. Although both probes were launched using a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, Voyager 1's launch occurred sixteen days after Voyager 2, despite the specific flight paths mandated by NASA. The core objective of this undertaking was a five-year observation period, which encompassed close observations of both Jupiter and Saturn. The alignment of these distant planets, which happens approximately every 176 years, was a rare and fortunate occurrence.

Voyager 1 arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this visit, the probe transmitted the first high-definition imagery of Jupiter's subtle rings and documented active volcanoes on its moon Io, establishing the initial record of extraterrestrial volcanism. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 executed its closest approach to Saturn. It gathered extremely detailed information regarding Saturn's ring structure and successfully photographed Titan. This path around Saturn propelled Voyager 1 beyond the plane of the solar system, concluding its planetary tour and setting a course toward interstellar expanse.

Following the suggestion of astronomer Carl Sagan, on February 14, 1990, Voyager 1 oriented its instruments back toward Earth, dispatching a single photograph while it was situated roughly 6 billion kilometers away. This solitary image, visible as one pixel against sunlight, earned the designation Pale Blue Dot. This transmission marked the final picture sent by the spacecraft before the cameras were shut down to conserve energy.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, after crossing the heliopause boundary, thus becoming the first human-made object to attain this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated based on Sagan’s advice. This record includes greetings in 55 languages, alongside visuals and music intended for any civilization that might eventually encounter it. Its power source is three radioisotope thermoelectric generators running on plutonium-238; the predictable decay of this isotope, possessing a half-life of 87.7 years, governs the power supply available.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated about 25 billion kilometers from Earth while traveling at a velocity near 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will achieve the significant milestone of covering a distance equivalent to one light-day.
output_text:
Voyager 1 began its mission from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. Although both probes utilized a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket for launch, Voyager 1 was launched sixteen days following Voyager 2, notwithstanding the specific trajectory profiles established by NASA. The primary goal of this endeavor was a five-year observation window, during which both Jupiter and Saturn were closely examined. The fortuitous alignment of these distant planets, occurring roughly every 176 years, was an uncommon and welcome cosmic coincidence.

Voyager 1 reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this visit, the probe transmitted the first high-resolution images of Jupiter's faint rings and documented active volcanoes on its moon Io, thereby establishing the inaugural record of volcanism beyond Earth. Two years afterward, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 made its closest pass to Saturn. It collected highly detailed data concerning Saturn's ring system and successfully photographed Titan. This passage by Saturn propelled Voyager 1 outside the plane of our solar system, concluding its planetary tour and setting a course toward interstellar depths.

Following a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 turned its instruments toward Earth on February 14, 1990, transmitting a solitary photograph while it was situated approximately 6 billion kilometers away. This single image, appearing as one pixel against the sunlight, earned the title Pale Blue Dot. This transmission represented the last picture transmitted by the spacecraft before the cameras were deactivated to conserve power.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, upon crossing the heliopause boundary, becoming the initial human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated based on Sagan’s counsel. This record contains greetings in 55 languages, alongside music and visuals intended for any civilization that might eventually encounter it. Its power source relies on three radioisotope thermoelectric generators powered by plutonium-238; the predictable decay of this isotope, with a half-life of 87.7 years, sustains the available power.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is located about 25 billion kilometers from Earth while maintaining a velocity approaching 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will reach the notable milestone of having traveled a distance equivalent to one light-day.
